imageRegistry.caption: Sagittal T1WI MR, part of a series in a patient with diabetic Charcot foot, shows numerous abnormalities. Charcot joints are noted at navicular cuneiforms . There is ↓ signal in the plantar soft tissues, with confluent ↓ signal in the adjacent cuboid , concerning for osteomyelitis. It also shows diffuse ↓ signals throughout the talus , an unexpected finding.

visualEvidence.duplicateEvidenceRole: Use this plain/unannotated preview for visual duplicate classification before archiving any image.

imageRegistry.caption: Sagittal STIR MR in the same patient further defines the talar abnormality as an insufficiency fracture of the dome .

visualEvidence.duplicateEvidenceRole: Use this plain/unannotated preview for visual duplicate classification before archiving any image.

imageRegistry.caption: Sagittal T1WI C+ FS MR, adjacent slice, shows a wedge-shaped region of hypointensity , representing a segment of osteonecrosis (ON) in the body of the talus. Remember that the talus is prone to ON and that the tarsal bones are prone to insufficiency fracture in diabetic feet. Sinus track led to the cuboid, confirming osteomyelitis, which was suspected based on both T1 and STIR.

entries.caption: Axial CT in a patient with Charcot shoulder shows an enormous effusion extending from the shoulder down around the proximal shaft of the humerus . The fluid contains abundant bone debris and could be mistaken for mineralized soft tissue masses.

entries.caption: Sagittal radiograph shows Charcot osteoarthropathy with disorganization of the Chopart (hindfoot-midfoot) joint . The patient is diabetic, and little debris is visible in this atrophic joint. There was a clinically unsuspected subacute fibula fracture as well.

entries.caption: AP radiograph of the knee shows fragmentation and a tense effusion, which decompresses down fascial planes of the leg. This decompression carries bony debris distal to the joint . This is a typical Charcot knee in a patient with acquired syphilis.

entries.caption: AP radiograph of the foot shows lateral dislocation of the metatarsals relative to the tarsals. The medial cortex of the 1st metatarsal should align perfectly with the medial cortex of the medial cuneiform. Vascular calcification is present. This is a Lisfranc dislocation resulting from diabetic neuropathy.
